plan: Sell all leveraged ETFs (SOXL, SPAL) to zero.
Trim semiconductor exposure (SMH) and broad index (QQQ) to lower risk.
Reduce SPY concentration to meet the 25% single name limit.
Increase allocation to income (QQQI) for stability.
Add a new high-quality, good risk/reward name (CVX).
Hold a larger cash buffer for safety.
conservative.read: This plan targets ~1.5% monthly return with capital preservation as the priority. It significantly reduces leverage and concentration risk. Expect lower volatility and drawdowns.

plan: Trim leveraged ETFs (SOXL) and remove others (SPAL).
Reduce exposure in semiconductors (SMH) and broad index (QQQ).
Cut SPY concentration to meet the 25% limit.
Increase position in a strong performer (CRWD).
Add a new high-scoring risk/reward name (GUSH).
Maintain a moderate cash buffer.
balanced.read: This plan aims for ~2.5% monthly return with a focus on risk-first growth. It balances broad index, quality tech, and some leverage. Expect moderate volatility.

plan: Increase allocation to leveraged ETFs (SOXL, SPAL) for aggressive growth.
Trim broad index (SPY) to manage concentration.
Add to strong tech performer (CRWD) and new top setups (GUSH, CVX).
Maintain a smaller cash buffer.
Utilize wider stops for potential higher returns but increased risk.
Accept higher potential drawdowns.
aggressive.read: This plan targets ~4% monthly return by leaning into leverage and high-conviction names. Expect significantly higher volatility and potential for large drawdowns due to leverage and ETF decay. Strict stop discipline is crucial.

plan: Sell all volatile single names and leveraged ETFs.
Eliminate exposure to semiconductors and broad index ETFs.
Maximize allocation to income-focused ETFs (QQQI).
Increase allocation to a stable broad index (SPY) for yield potential.
Prioritize reliable monthly distributions over growth.
Maintain a significant cash buffer.
income.read: This plan targets ~1% monthly return focused on generating steady income. It minimizes volatility and capital risk by holding income-generating assets. NAV can still fall in market downturns.
